planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; atemoya is a hybrid of sugar apple and cherimoya suited to zones 10–11, where it produces some of the most richly flavored tropical fruits available to home growers. Hand-pollination in the morning significantly increases fruit set.

directions: Plant in fertile, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 10–11; atemoya is a man-made hybrid between the sugar apple (Annona squamosa) and the cherimoya (Annona cherimola) that captures the best qualities of both parents — a large, greenish, bumpy-skinned fruit with exceptionally sweet, creamy white flesh that many tasters rank among the finest of all tropical fruits. Because the two parent species flower at different times, natural pollination can be unreliable; hand-pollinate by collecting pollen from male-stage flowers in the morning and dusting it onto female-stage flowers on the same or another tree to ensure heavy crops. Trees begin bearing within 2–5 years of transplanting and can produce substantial crops once established in a warm, frost-free location. Protect young trees from any frost, as even light freezes can cause severe damage or death.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Plant in spring in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun; Brazilian guava is more cold-tolerant than common guava (Psidium guajava) and can survive brief frosts in the warmer parts of zone 9. It tolerates a range of soil conditions and is notably resistant to guava wilt disease, making it valuable in areas where common guava struggles.

directions: Plant in well-drained, moderately fertile soil in full sun in zones 9–11; Brazilian guava (also called cas in Costa Rica) is a small to medium-sized tree native to tropical America that produces heavy crops of small, round fruits with white, aromatic, pleasantly sour flesh — highly prized throughout Latin America for fresh-squeezed juice, fruit nectars, jellies, fermented beverages, and agua fresca. Unlike common guava, it shows considerable resistance to the devastating guava wilt fungus (Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. psidii) and is more tolerant of occasional frosts, making it a practical choice for gardeners at the cool edge of the tropical fruit range in zones 9–10. Trees begin producing in 3–6 years from transplant and fruit prolifically once established, often producing two crops per year in warm climates. Fruits drop quickly when ripe and should be harvested promptly or they will be taken by wildlife.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full sun; cambucá is a rainforest understory tree from Brazil's Atlantic Forest that tolerates and even prefers some afternoon shade, making it a candidate for food forest mid-canopy positions. Use fresh seed, as viability drops sharply within a few weeks of extraction.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in partial to full sun in zones 10–11; cambucá is a rare Atlantic Forest gem in the Myrtaceae family (closely related to jaboticaba) that produces large, round, yellow-green fruits directly on its trunk and major branches — a phenomenon known as cauliflory that makes the tree a striking spectacle when loaded with fruit. The thick-skinned fruits hold exceptionally sweet, white, juicy flesh with a fragrant, complex flavor described as a blend of mango and passion fruit, and are eaten fresh or made into artisanal jams and wines in the Brazilian coastal states where it is native. Cambucá prefers the high humidity and warm temperatures of its coastal rainforest home and performs best with regular deep watering and organic mulch kept away from the trunk. Seed must be planted fresh — sow immediately after extraction from ripe fruit, as viability falls dramatically within two to three weeks.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil in full sun; jaboticaba is a slow-growing Brazilian native that ultimately rewards patience with a spectacular display of purple, grape-like fruits blanketing the trunk and branches. Established trees tolerate brief frosts to around 26°F and can persist in the warmest microclimates of zone 9b.

directions: Plant in fertile, slightly acidic, well-drained soil amended with compost in full sun in zones 9–11; jaboticaba is one of the most visually arresting fruit trees in the world, producing dense clusters of dark purple, grape-like fruits that emerge directly from the smooth, cinnamon-colored bark of the trunk and main limbs — an otherworldly sight when the tree is in heavy fruit. The fruits are sweet and rich, eaten fresh by the handful, and also made into wines, liqueurs, and jams in Brazil, but they ferment rapidly and must be processed or consumed within three to four days of harvest. Jaboticaba is extremely slow growing, typically requiring 8–15 years to reach first fruiting from seed, though the wait is rewarded with a tree that can fruit multiple times per year in warm climates once it matures. Maintain consistently moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil and apply organic mulch to retain soil moisture, as the tree is intolerant of drought when young.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to light shade; rambai is a lowland Southeast Asian rainforest tree that thrives in the consistently warm, humid conditions of zones 10–11. Plant only in frost-free locations, as the tree has little cold tolerance and is killed by temperatures below freezing.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun in zones 10–11; rambai is a medium-sized Southeast Asian fruit tree in the Phyllanthaceae family that produces large, pendulous clusters of small, grape-like fruits — ranging in color from pale yellow to deep pink or red — borne on the branches and trunk in the cauliflorous habit shared by many tropical rainforest species. The translucent, sweet-tart flesh surrounds relatively large seeds and is eaten fresh or made into juices, wines, and pickles across Myanmar, Bangladesh, Thailand, and Malaysia, where the fruit is a beloved seasonal treat sold in local markets. Trees are dioecious, meaning separate male and female trees are required for fruit production, so plant at least two seedlings or obtain a confirmed female tree grafted onto a rootstock. Maintain high humidity and consistent moisture for best growth, and apply a thick layer of organic mulch to keep the root zone cool and moist throughout the year.
sources: USDA ARS National Plant Germplasm System
Morton, J. Fruits of Warm Climates
